ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Mutton Do Pyaza; mapped to lamb dish as the closest defensible parent class. Mutton (mature sheep) is the primary protein component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Mutton Do Pyaza; A0FJP (sheep meat, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor for the primary protein component in this composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Mutton Do Pyaza or a sufficiently close composite Indian mutton dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mutton Do Pyaza is approximately 250 g, representing a standard restaurant or home-cooked portion of the braised mutton and onion curry served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Do Pyaza — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Do Pyaza (cr_id G00010722). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb shoulder (USDA NDB 17290) as the closest approved-source analogue; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Do Pyaza. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the analogous food category of mixed meat-and-onion curry dishes with low net carbohydrate content (~7.4 g/100g). The dominant carbohydrate contributors are onion and tomato, both of which have low GI values (onion GI ~10, tomato GI ~15 per University of Sydney GI Database). The composite dish GI is estimated conservatively at 35. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 2.5) / 100 = 6.5 g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg per 100 g; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 1168 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or papadums; the double-onion component provides modest potassium (210 mg/100g) but is insufficient to offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (7.44 g/100g), low sugar (4.69 g/100g), high protein (14.05 g/100g), and estimated low GI (~35) support glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Do Pyaza is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions (150–250 g) without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the high protein content promotes satiety and attenuates postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.5 g/100g estimated) and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) from mutton, combined with high sodium (467 mg/100g), present a moderate cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Mutton Do Pyaza to occasional consumption (once per week or less), choose lean cuts, request preparation with minimal ghee or oil, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which drives pro-inflammatory cytokine production; tomato and red chili are nightshade vegetables that may exacerbate symptoms in susceptible individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise Mutton Do Pyaza due to its red meat content and nightshade ingredients; if consumed, monitor individual symptom response and consider substituting mutton with a leaner, lower arachidonic-acid protein source.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Do Pyaza is a classic North Indian braised mutton curry distinguished by its double quantity of onions — 'do pyaza' literally means 'two onions' in Hindi/Urdu, referring to onions added at two stages of cooking to build layered sweetness and body. The dish is prepared by slow-cooking bone-in or boneless mutton pieces with a rich masala base of ginger, garlic, tomato, yogurt, cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chili, and garam masala, with onions incorporated both early (for the sauce base) and late (for texture). The result is a deeply flavoured, moderately thick curry with tender mutton, a slightly tangy yogurt-enriched sauce, and a pronounced savoury-spiced aroma. In Singapore, Mutton Do Pyaza is served in Indian restaurants and hawker stalls, typically accompanied by basmati rice, roti prata, or naan. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, and 7.44 g carbohydrate, with 467 mg sodium and 85 mg cholesterol. The dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate and low GI profile, but requires sodium moderation for hypertension and should be consumed in limited frequency by those with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat, cholesterol, and arachidonic acid content.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Do Pyaza (cr_id G00010722) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from the closest USDA SR Legacy analogue (cooked lamb shoulder) and documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database principles, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb shoulder (NDB 17290) as the HPB record does not report saturated fat. This is an approved-source adaptation and is documented in nutrient_source. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional composite Indian mutton dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2026. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Do Pyaza was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 4. Halal status is assumed based on the dish's Indian Muslim culinary tradition and typical Singapore hawker preparation; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 5. Chinese local names (羊肉双葱咖喱 / 羊肉雙蔥咖喱) are descriptive translations used in Singapore Chinese-language menus; no single universally standardised Chinese name exists for this dish.
